retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Mechanism -- Acute Rejection
retrieved_docs.content: Acute rejection develops within weeks to months. The rejection involves the activation of T lymphocytes against donor MHC. This reaction may also involve a humoral immune response when antibodies develop after transplant. The inflammation occurs as vasculitis of graft vessels with dense interstitial lymphocytic infiltrate.

retrieved_docs.title: Managing Fresh-Frozen Plasma Transfusion Adverse Effects: Allergic Reactions, TACO, and TRALI -- Pathophysiology -- Acute Transfusion Reactions
retrieved_docs.content: In transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI), the primary mechanism involves generating and activating neutrophils within the pulmonary endothelium. Two main pathophysiological mechanisms are implicated in TRALI: antibody-mediated and nonantibody-mediated (see Image. Pathophysiology of TRALI). Both mechanisms can lead to acute lung injury following a blood transfusion. A 2-hit hypothesis often explains TRALI. The first hit is the preexisting clinical condition of the patient, which may prime or sensitize the pulmonary endothelium and neutrophils. The second hit is the transfusion of blood or blood components, which can activate these primed neutrophils, ultimately leading to the development of acute lung injury. This dual mechanism underscores the complexity of TRALI's pathophysiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Reperfusion Injury -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanisms of injury involved in vascular reperfusion are three-fold: Increased formation of reactive oxygen species Microvascular vasoconstriction Adhesion of neutrophils to endothelial lining, their activation, and release of cytokines.

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Physiology -- Mechanism -- Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory cells migrate to the wound site after platelet activation during the first several days following injury. Mast cells release vasoactive cytokines such as prostaglandins and histamine which increase capillary permeability and promote local dilation to aid the migratory process.

retrieved_docs.title: Restenosis of Stented Coronary Arteries -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Restenosis may occur as a result of an excessive reaction to the vascular injury sustained from the PCI. The first vascular response following the trauma of PCI includes elastic recoil, platelet plug, and thrombus formation, occurring in the initial minutes and hours following intervention. This initial response is followed by a complex inflammatory and reparative process that lasts weeks to months or even years in the case of DES-ISR. The acute phase is characterized by the deposition of platelets and fibrin, with the adhesion of circulating neutrophils and monocytes. Over weeks, chronic inflammatory cells (macrophages and giant cells) will gradually replace the acute inflammatory cells at the site of vascular injury. These processes occur over a relatively short period (weeks to months), unlike the slow progression (years) of atherosclerotic disease in native coronary lesions. The two principal biologic mechanisms of ISR are neointimal hyperplasia and neoatherosclerosis. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Pathology, Inflammation -- Issues of Concern -- Acute or Chronic Inflammation
retrieved_docs.content: Cryotherapy is an effective treatment for acute inflammation caused by musculoskeletal injury, leading to decreased pain and more rapid  return to participation.  [8] The primary mechanism is reduced sensory nerve transmission at the site, whereas peripheral vasoconstriction slows the metabolic rate. Reducing oxidative stress reduces secondary ischemic injury and alleviates inflammation. [9] Cryotherapy is most effective when applied soon after the injury, within 24 to 48 hours. [10]  Chronic inflammation has a slow onset of days, a long duration of years, less prominent classical signs and symptoms, and cellular infiltrate composed of monocytes, macrophages, and lymphocytes. [11] Chronic inflammation can arise from persistent infections caused by microorganisms that are difficult to eliminate, such as mycobacteria, viruses, fungi, and parasites, triggering delayed-type hypersensitivity reactions that may lead to granulomatous responses or unresolved acute inflammation evolving into a chronic state. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Vascular Reperfusion Injury -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There is evidence to state that ROS formed by one enzymatic pathway goes onto activate and speed up the production of more ROS by other pathways. The rate at which ROS are formed exceeds the rate at which they can be detoxified. These ROS then go onto activate neutrophils. [8] Adherence of these neutrophils to endothelial lining causes the release of cytokines and damage to the membrane along with activation of the entire inflammatory and complement cascade.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Delayed Radiation Injury -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Two mechanisms of injury have been proposed and supported by animal and in vitro studies. The first postulates direct damage to small vessel endothelium, being a tissue that exhibits rapid turnover, through interaction with the radiation-induced reactive oxidative species. The resulting debris interrupts the vascular flow, a process known as endarteritis obliterans. The other model describes a delayed process initiated at the time of radiation with the release of bioactive, fibrogenic cytokines inhibiting parenchymal and stem cells and causing the extensive fibrosis seen in several damaged irradiated tissue. This is known as the fibro-atrophic effect. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Splenic Injury(Archived) -- Pathophysiology -- Immunological Functions
retrieved_docs.content: Contribution to immunity: The spleen is involved in both humoral immunity and cell-mediated immunity, actively contributing to the body's defense mechanisms. Particulate removal: The spleen operates as a refined filter, removing particulates, opsonized bacteria, and antibody-coated cells from the bloodstream.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Transplantation Rejection(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The 2011 Banff meeting focused on diagnostic criteria for late antibody-mediated rejection. During this review, CD4-negative antibody-mediated rejection was recognized, and endothelial activation and natural killer (NK) cells were identified as primary contributors to chronic rejection. [4] Other proposed pathophysiological mechanisms include: Calcineurin inhibitors toxicity Chronic dysfunction of the solid organ causing premature graft loss Alloimmunization to minor HLA antigens Production of interferon-γ and interleukin-2 by T H 1 lymphocytes Secretion of cytokines by endothelial cells

retrieved_docs.title: Wound Grafts -- Complications -- Early Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Graft rejection While rare in autografts, rejection can occur in allografts or composite grafts in a delayed fashion, often due to immune-mediated mechanisms. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Treatment of Brain Radiation Necrosis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Pathologically, radiation necrosis primarily affects the smaller arterioles and arteries which initially causes coagulative necrosis and then endothelial thickening and infiltration of lymphocytes and macrophages. The inflammation that develops from the infiltration of these immune cells triggers off a whole host of cytokine activity, activation of fibroblasts, and hyalinization of the tissue.  Immune-mediated mechanisms that contribute to neurotoxicity have been hypothesized but have not been fully explained.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Immune Response -- Mechanism -- Hyperacute Rejection
retrieved_docs.content: In hyperacute rejection, the transplanted tissue is rejected within minutes to hours because vascularization is rapidly destroyed. Hyperacute rejection is antibody-mediated and occurs because the recipient has preexisting antibodies against the graft, possibly due to prior blood transfusions, multiple pregnancies, prior transplantation, or xenografts. Activation of the complement system leads to thrombosis in the vessels, preventing the vascularization of the graft.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Granulation Tissue -- Mechanism -- Inflammatory Stage
retrieved_docs.content: Monocytes arrive at the injury site within 2 days, differentiating into macrophages. The role of macrophages is to phagocytose debris, pathogens, and neutrophils while also producing chemical mediators (TGF-β and VEGF) that recruit fibroblasts and endothelial cells and mark the next stage of healing. During this stage, the site of injury becomes erythematous and edematous. This stage takes about 72 hours to complete.

retrieved_docs.title: Sympathetic Ophthalmia -- Pathophysiology -- The Mechanism of Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: The precise pathophysiology of sympathetic ophthalmia is not clearly understood; however, most evidence points to an autoimmune inflammatory process secondary to ocular injury following trauma or surgery. [34] The eye is an immune-privileged space in the body where systemic immune responses to antigens are markedly reduced. This is achieved by the absence of intraocular lymphatics and tight junctions in the retinal pigment epithelium, creating the blood-retina barrier. [35] Disruption of this system in penetrating injuries results in exposure of previously sequestered ocular antigens to local conjunctival lymphatics, activating cell-mediated immunity and a type IV delayed hypersensitivity reaction. [36]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 239
retrieved_docs.content: classifications, co‑occurrence patterns, and shared pathological mechanisms such as tissue damage and neurotoxicity. Recognizing these interrelationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, coding, and management of patients who sustain complex traumatic injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2041
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia, non‑regenerative anemia, arthritis, hemolytic anemia that is not caused by autoimmunity, and coronary artery inflammation are all classified within the broader category of immune disorders/autoimmune conditions. This grouping reflects the shared underlying involvement of the immune system in the pathogenesis of these diverse diseases, whether through direct autoimmune attack on blood‑forming tissues, inflammatory processes affecting joints, or vascular inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing these entities as members of the immune/autoimmune disorder spectrum helps clinicians consider common immunologic mechanisms, guide diagnostic evaluation, and select therapeutic strategies that target immune dysregulation across these seemingly disparate clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 867
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest typically results from a traumatic event and is characterized by tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة). This tissue damage may be accompanied by neurotoxic changes (سمية عصبية). An injury described as “injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of motor vehicle” is classified as a Motor vehicle traffic (MVT) injury and shares the same associated morphology of tissue damage (تَلَف الأنسجة). Both the general internal injury of chest and the motor‑vehicle‑related injury are linked through the common pathological finding of tissue damage, which can involve neurotoxicity, underscoring the need to evaluate the traumatic mechanism and potential toxic effects in patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2027
retrieved_docs.content: Open wounds (جرح مفتوح) are clinically associated with crushing injuries or internal injuries, indicating that such traumatic mechanisms often result in an open wound presentation. Within the category of open wounds, cut wounds (جرح قطع) represent a specific subtype, meaning that a cut wound is a particular form of open wound. Recognizing that crushing or internal injuries can lead to open wounds, and that cut wounds are a distinct subset of these, aids clinicians in diagnosing the nature of the trauma and selecting appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: The phrase “Due to” is associated with crushing injury or internal injury, indicating that these types of injuries are frequently described as occurring because of specific causative events. This association highlights the importance of identifying the underlying mechanisms when evaluating patients with crushing or internal injuries, as the wording “due to” often signals a direct link between the injury and its precipitating factor.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2447
retrieved_docs.content: Necrotizing glomerulonephritis is classified as a form of اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (glomerular disease) and is frequently encountered as a component of متلازمة رئوية كلوية (pulmonary‑renal syndrome). As a severe, necrotizing inflammation of the renal glomeruli, it represents a specific subtype within the broader category of glomerular pathology. Its association with pulmonary‑renal syndrome highlights the clinical pattern in which simultaneous lung hemorrhage and rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis occur, often due to small‑vessel vasculitis or autoimmune mechanisms. Recognizing necrotizing glomerulonephritis as both a distinct glomerular disease and a manifestation of pulmonary‑renal syndrome is essential for accurate diagnosis, prompt immunosuppressive treatment, and coordinated care of the intertwined renal and pulmonary complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 713
retrieved_docs.content: The category “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions, including فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (hemolytic anemia not caused by autoimmunity), التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). These entities are grouped together because they involve immune system dysregulation, whether through autoantibody‑mediated attacks, cell‑mediated immune injury, or broader immune‑related inflammatory processes. While most of the listed disorders are driven by autoimmune mechanisms, the non‑autoimmune hemolytic anemia is included in the classification for organizational purposes despite its different pathogenesis. Recognizing that these diverse diseases share an underlying immune component helps clinicians identify common diagnostic pathways and consider immunomodulatory therapies across different organ systems, thereby improving patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2562
retrieved_docs.content: Injury due to legal intervention by firearm and injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted by crashing of aircraft are both linked to the morphological finding of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة is itself associated with سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that such traumatic events may involve neurotoxic processes alongside structural tissue injury. Recognizing the connection between these mechanisms can inform clinical assessment and management of patients with firearm‑related legal interventions or aircraft‑related trauma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 147
retrieved_docs.content: nephropathy) and is linked to داء مضاد للغشاء القاعدي الكبيجي and متلازمة رئوية كلوية, indicating an immune‑mediated mechanism with possible pulmonary involvement. اعتلال كبيبات الكلى also includes اليوريمية as a downstream consequence of glomerular injury. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how ocular hemorrhagic findings, venous and lymphatic disorders, cardiovascular diseases, and glomerular nephropathies are organized within shared vascular and circulatory frameworks, highlighting common pathogenic pathways, systemic associations, and therapeutic considerations such as mesna use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1998
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory morphology is a characteristic tissue pattern that is associated with coronary atherosclerosis and other forms of heart disease, indicating that inflammatory changes play a central role in the development and progression of these cardiovascular conditions. The same inflammatory morphological features are also observed in senile arteritis, linking age‑related arterial inflammation to the broader spectrum of heart disease pathology. Recognizing inflammatory morphology across coronary atherosclerosis, other heart diseases, and senile arteritis underscores the importance of inflammation as a common underlying mechanism in vascular disorders and can inform diagnostic and therapeutic strategies aimed at mitigating inflammatory damage in the cardiovascular system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 1252
retrieved_docs.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي), cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)). These conditions share a common underlying mechanism of immune system dysregulation that leads to tissue‑specific damage, whether in the bone marrow, red blood cells, joints, or coronary vessels. Recognizing their placement within the autoimmune spectrum aids clinicians in identifying shared pathogenic pathways, selecting appropriate immunomodulatory therapies, and monitoring for related autoimmune comorbidities.

Vascular surgeons perform procedures such as aortic catheter insertion and wound‑crust removal, linking procedural expertise to the management of burn and infection debridement. These interconnected classifications support coherent medical documentation, diagnosis, and patient‑care planning by linking traumatic mechanisms, injury subtypes, associated pathologies, and specialty‑specific interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1165
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is classified as a traumatic event that can serve as a cause of subsequent pathology. Both internal chest injury and an injury undetermined whether accidentally or purposely inflicted, by crashing of aircraft are described as having the associated morphology of تَلَف الأنسجة (tissue damage). The presence of تَلَف الأنسجة is further linked to سمية عصبية (neurotoxicity), indicating that tissue damage in these contexts may involve neurotoxic mechanisms. Recognizing the relationship between chest trauma, tissue damage, and potential neurotoxic effects is important for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 157

Finally, إصابة حادة في الدماغ (acute brain injury) is considered a form of اعتلال دماغي, reinforcing the concept that acute traumatic or ischemic insults to the mozek constitute a type of brain disease. Together, these relationships illustrate how various imaging findings, inflammatory, metabolic, vascular, toxic, and traumatic processes converge on the mozek, linking structural abnormalities, physiological functions, and clinical syndromes within an integrated framework of brain health and disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis is clinically similar to hypertension with complications and secondary hypertension, indicating that the severe renal vascular changes seen in malignant arteriolar nephrosclerosis share comparable clinical manifestations, risk factors, and disease mechanisms with complicated primary hypertension and hypertension that arises secondary to other underlying conditions. This similarity underscores the importance of recognizing overlapping signs and symptoms when diagnosing and managing patients with advanced hypertensive organ damage and secondary causes of elevated blood pressure.
